message.content:
You are verifier #1, independent of all other verifiers. Do NOT confirm findings optimistically — only confirm if you can independently verify the issue exists in the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

Finding F-051: "Bearer token scrubbing regex misses base64 characters (+ and /) — partial redaction leaves token fragments"
File: scripts/capture_transcripts.py (line 39)
Description: Bearer token redaction pattern [A-Za-z0-9._\-]{20,} missing + and /. Base64 tokens split at first + or /, trailing portion remains in transcript.
PoC: [REDACTED]: retains +iOiJIUzI1NiJ9.payload/signature after redaction.

Instructions:
1. Read the actual file at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py
2. Look at the code at line 39 and surrounding context
3. Independently verify whether the described problem EXISTS in the current code
4. Check if the PoC is reproducible from the actual code as written
5. Consider whether this is already fixed (pass-1 #101-#122, pass-2 #124-#156 fixed many issues)
6. Return your independent verdict

Confirmed = true ONLY if: (a) the code problem exists as described, (b) the PoC is reproducible from current code, (c) it is a genuine production-readiness concern. Set confirmed=false for speculative issues, issues already fixed, or issues where the PoC does not match what the code actually does.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-thomasjones-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense; does not touch paths/email/reasoning, which are
34	# legitimate provenance — see README caveat for the public-release consideration).
35	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
36	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
37	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
38	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
39	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-]{20,}"),
40	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
41	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
42	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
43	]
44	
45	
46	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
47	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
48	        if pat.groups == 2:
49	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
50	        elif pat.groups == 1:
51	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
52	        else:
53	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
54	    return text
55	
56	
57	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
58	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
59	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
60	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
61	
62	
63	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
64	    try:
65	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
66	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
67	        if srcf.suffix == ".jsonl":
68	            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
69	        else:
70	            data = raw
71	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
72	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
73	        else:
74	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
75	        return {
76	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
77	            "bytes": len(data),
78	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
79	        }
80	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit

content.text:
The file is read. Line 39 contains the Bearer token regex. Let me verify the exact pattern.

The regex at line 39 is:
```python
re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-]{20,}"),
```

The character class `[A-Za-z0-9._\-]` does not include `+` or `/`, which are valid base64 characters. JWT tokens and other base64-encoded bearer tokens commonly contain these characters.

The PoC is directly reproducible: a token like `[REDACTED]` would match only up to the first `+`, redacting `Bearer eyJhbGc` as `[REDACTED]` but leaving `+iOiJIUzI1NiJ9.payload/signature` in plain text in the transcript.

This is confirmed as a real issue in the current code. The fix would be to add `+/` to the character class, or use a broader pattern like `[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}` or `\S{20,}` (non-whitespace).

input.reasoning: Line 39 of /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py contains: re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-]{20,}"). The character class [A-Za-z0-9._\-] omits + and /, which are standard base64 alphabet characters. JWTs and many OAuth bearer tokens encode their payload in base64url or standard base64 and frequently contain these characters. The regex match stops at the first + or / encountered, so the portion of the token after that character is left unredacted in the captured transcript. The PoC is directly reproducible from the code as written: a token like eyJhbGc+iOiJIUzI1NiJ9.payload/signature would result in only "Bearer eyJhbGc" being replaced with [REDACTED], leaving "+iOiJIUzI1NiJ9.payload/signature" exposed. This is a genuine production-readiness concern because transcripts are committed to the repo by the pre-commit hook, and partial token fragments can still be exploitable or used to reconstruct secrets. The issue does not appear to have been fixed in prior hardening passes — the pattern is still literally [A-Za-z0-9._\-]{20,} with no + or / included.
